title: Does CLIP’s generalization performance mainly stem from high train-test similarity?

reviews.summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.
reviews.strengths: The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.
reviews.weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.
reviews.questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.

Strengths:
The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.

Weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.

Questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Understanding CLIP's Generalization: Beyond Train-Test Similarity"

**Summary of Contribution:**
This paper investigates the hypothesis that CLIP's strong out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization performance, particularly on benchmarks like ImageNet variants, is primarily driven by high similarity between its training data (LAION) and these test sets. Through a series of carefully designed pruning experiments on LAION, the authors demonstrate that while train-test similarity contributes, CLIP's OOD performance remains robust even after removing highly similar images. This suggests that other properties of the large-scale, diverse LAION dataset, such as its sheer scale and diversity, play crucial and potentially dominant roles in enabling CLIP's generalization capabilities. The core finding challenges the notion that similarity is the primary driver.

**Strengths:**
1.  **Clarity and Motivation:** The paper is well-written, presenting a clear motivation for investigating the drivers behind CLIP's OOD generalization. The central hypothesis regarding train-test similarity is articulated concisely.
2.  **Rigorous Experimental Design:** The methodology is a significant strength. The authors employ a rigorous approach, defining the "similarity gap" using nearest-neighbor distances based on CLIP ViT-B/16+ embeddings and systematically pruning LAION splits to replicate ImageNet's similarity gap to various benchmarks. This provides a direct and controlled test of the similarity hypothesis.

**Weaknesses and Limitations:**
1.  **Limited Benchmark Scope:** A major limitation is that the analysis and experiments are confined exclusively to ImageNet-variant datasets (e.g., ImageNet-V2, ImageNet-R, ImageNet-Sketch). While these are common OOD benchmarks, the findings' generalizability to fundamentally different types of OOD data, such as those found in specialized domains (e.g., satellite imagery in FMoW or wildlife imagery in iWILDCam), remains unvalidated and uncertain. This narrow scope raises questions about whether the conclusions hold broadly.
2.  **Lack of Non-ImageNet OOD Validation:** Directly stemming from the first point, the paper lacks any validation on established non-ImageNet OOD benchmarks. Testing the core finding on datasets like iWILDCam or FMoW would significantly strengthen the claims about the role of scale/diversity versus similarity beyond the ImageNet ecosystem.

**Main Points and Methodology:**
*   The central finding is that CLIP's OOD performance cannot be fully explained by train-test similarity; other dataset properties inherent to LAION (scale, diversity) are essential contributors.
*   This is evidenced by the key experimental result: pruning LAION to match the similarity gap observed between ImageNet-1k training and its variants causes only marginal performance drops on those variants. Performance remains substantially higher than models trained on ImageNet-1k itself.
*   The methodology hinges on using CLIP embeddings (ViT-B/16+) to measure perceptual similarity via nearest-neighbor distances between training (LAION splits) and test sets. The defined "similarity gap" quantifies the difference in these distances compared to a reference (ImageNet-1k train to its test). Pruning strategies are then applied to LAION to manipulate this gap.
*   The analysis being limited to ImageNet-based test sets is a significant caveat regarding the generalizability of the conclusions.

**Overall Assessment and Recommendation:**
This paper presents a valuable and well-executed investigation into the drivers of CLIP's generalization. The core experiment rigorously testing the similarity hypothesis via dataset pruning is compelling within its defined scope (ImageNet variants). The finding that similarity alone cannot explain CLIP's performance, implicating scale and diversity, is an important contribution to understanding large-scale vision-language models. However, the significant limitation is the exclusive focus on ImageNet-variant benchmarks, leaving the generalizability of the conclusions to other OOD scenarios unexplored and unproven. This narrow scope prevents the work from having a broader impact at this stage.

**Decision:**
**6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

The paper's rigorous methodology and clear demonstration challenging the primacy of similarity for ImageNet-variant OOD performance constitute a solid contribution worthy of publication. However, the lack of validation on diverse, non-ImageNet OOD benchmarks limits the scope and broader significance of the findings, placing it marginally above the acceptance threshold. Addressing this limitation in future work would significantly strengthen the conclusions.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.
reviews.questions: What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Investigating the Role of Train-Test Similarity in CLIP's Out-of-Distribution Generalization"

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a timely investigation into the source of CLIP's robust out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization capabilities. The central hypothesis questions whether high train-test distribution similarity within the LAION-400M dataset is the primary driver. The authors employ a novel approach by filtering out samples deemed similar to OOD test sets and demonstrate that CLIP maintains strong performance. A particularly intriguing finding is that a carefully curated 100M subset of LAION-400M can train a high-performing model. While the experimental design is well-motivated and the core findings are valuable, the scope of the evaluation is limited, and several methodological details require clarification. Overall, the work offers a novel perspective but needs refinement to fully substantiate its conclusions.

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Novel Perspective:** The paper provides a valuable and novel perspective by directly challenging the assumption that CLIP's OOD generalization primarily stems from high similarity between training (LAION-400M) and test distributions. This is a significant contribution to understanding foundation model generalization.
2.  **Compelling Core Experiment:** The core experiment, which involves pruning nearest neighbor samples from LAION relative to OOD test sets and showing retained strong CLIP performance, is well-designed and effectively supports the main conclusion that factors beyond simple train-test similarity are crucial.
3.  **Intriguing Subset Finding:** The demonstration that a high-performing model can be trained on a much smaller (100M) subset of LAION-400M is a noteworthy and practically relevant result, suggesting potential pathways for more efficient training.

**Weaknesses and Concerns:**  
1.  **Limited Benchmark Scope:** The evaluation is confined solely to classification tasks (e.g., ImageNet variants). This significantly limits the generalizability of the findings. CLIP is widely used in other critical domains like cross-modal retrieval, and the core hypothesis should be tested in these settings (e.g., using MSCOCO for filtering and Flickr-30k for OOD retrieval testing).
2.  **Insufficient Methodological Detail:** A critical weakness is the lack of essential details regarding the construction of the nearest neighbor sets used for filtering (Section 4.1). Key parameters such as the *number of nearest neighbors considered per test sample* and the specific *CLIP score thresholds* applied for inclusion/exclusion are omitted, hindering reproducibility and a full understanding of the filtering rigor.
3.  **Unresolved Sample Density Concern:** The paper inadequately addresses the potential confounder that LAION-400M, even after filtering nearest neighbors, might inherently possess a much higher *density* of samples near any given test sample compared to standard datasets like ImageNet-Train. While the closest sample is removed, many other samples might reside at a similar distance. The paper does not validate whether the density near test sets in the filtered LAION is comparable to that in ImageNet-Train, potentially giving the filtered LAION model an unfair advantage in "OOD" generalization that isn't solely due to superior representation learning. This casts some doubt on the fairness of the comparison underlying the main conclusion.
4.  **Lack of Benchmark Diversity:** Relying only on ImageNet-related benchmarks is insufficient. Incorporating more diverse OOD benchmarks, such as the VTAB suite (as used in related work like DataComp), would provide a more comprehensive assessment of generalization capabilities.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1.  **Expand Evaluation Domains:** Crucially, extend the experiments to include cross-modal retrieval tasks. For example, perform nearest neighbor filtering on LAION using the MSCOCO validation set and evaluate OOD generalization on the Flickr30k test set. This is essential to demonstrate the broader applicability of the findings.
2.  **Incorporate Diverse Benchmarks:** Evaluate the models on the VTAB benchmark suite to assess generalization across a wider variety of tasks and domains.
3.  **Clarify Methodological Details:** Provide explicit details on the nearest neighbor filtering process: the number of neighbors (k-NN) considered per test sample and the CLIP score similarity thresholds used for inclusion/exclusion. This is vital for reproducibility.
4.  **Validate Sample Density:** Conduct an analysis to compare the density of training samples near the test sets *after filtering* between the used LAION subset and a standard reference dataset like ImageNet-Train (e.g., using k-NN distances for k>1). This is necessary to rule out the potential advantage mentioned in Weakness #3 and strengthen the core claim.

**Recommendation:**  
This paper tackles an important question regarding the source of CLIP's generalization with a compelling core experiment and a novel perspective. The finding that a high-performing model can be trained on a significantly smaller subset of LAION is also valuable. However, the limited scope of the evaluation (only classification), the omission of key methodological details, and the unresolved concern regarding potential sample density advantages in the filtered LAION dataset prevent the conclusions from being fully substantiated in their current form. Addressing the major suggestions, particularly expanding the evaluation to retrieval tasks and validating sample density, would significantly strengthen the work. Considering the novel perspective, the intriguing core result, and the potential impact, I recommend **acceptance** provided the authors can address the key weaknesses, especially points 1 and 3 under Weaknesses and the corresponding Suggestions 1 and 4. The paper is **marginally above the acceptance threshold** in its present state, contingent on satisfactory revision.

**Rating:** 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary:

However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. 2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. reviews.summary: The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.
reviews.strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"
reviews.questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Investigating the Source of CLIP's Out-of-Distribution Performance via Systematic Pruning"

**Summary:** This paper presents a thorough investigation into whether CLIP's superior out-of-distribution (OOD) performance stems primarily from high similarity between its training data (LAION-400M) and common OOD test sets. The core approach involves systematically pruning training images similar to test set samples and evaluating performance degradation. While the key finding—that CLIP's OOD advantage persists significantly even after substantial similarity reduction—is valuable and insightful, the methodology for establishing a *truly* OOD evaluation setting is a major limitation. The work is computationally intensive and provides useful resources, but the claims about OOD robustness require stronger validation.

**Strengths:**
1.  **Key Insight:** The central finding—that reducing image-level similarity between training (LAION) and test sets (like ImageNet-R/Sketch) significantly diminishes but does *not* eliminate CLIP's OOD performance advantage over standard ImageNet-trained models—is a valuable and non-obvious contribution. This persistence suggests factors beyond simple train-test leakage contribute to CLIP's robustness.
2.  **Rigor and Resources:** The experimental design is systematic and computationally demanding. The effort to prune large datasets based on CLIP embeddings is substantial. Releasing the pruned datasets and code is a significant benefit to the research community, enabling further investigation and replication.
3.  **Community Contribution:** Providing the pruned datasets and codebase is a commendable practice that enhances reproducibility and facilitates future research in this area.

**Weaknesses and Major Concerns:**
1.  **Inadequate True OOD Evaluation:** The primary methodological flaw is that the pruning strategy does not create a genuinely out-of-distribution test scenario. Removing only the *nearest neighbors* of specific test samples (e.g., specific ImageNet-R images) does not equate to removing the *entire test domain* (e.g., all images with a "sketch" style) from the training data. This is a critical oversight.
2.  **Residual Domain Information:** By only removing specific images similar to test samples, the pruned training set inevitably retains numerous other images belonging to the *same domain* as the test set (e.g., sketches of classes *not* in the specific test set, like ImageNet classes or other objects). These residual domain-specific samples (e.g., sketches of "space shuttles" when testing on ImageNet-R sketches) can act as a bridge, allowing the model to learn domain-invariant features and significantly undermining claims about OOD generalization. Performance may still heavily rely on domain-specific cues present in the training data.
3.  **Domain Leakage:** The evaluation cannot be considered truly OOD because the training data (even after pruning) almost certainly contains other images from the test domains (e.g., sketch-style images of non-ImageNet classes). This leakage fundamentally weakens the conclusion that the observed performance stems from robust OOD generalization rather than residual domain-specific information.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**
1.  **Clarity of Contributions:** The abstract and contributions section could be significantly improved by providing a more concise and focused summary of the key findings, particularly emphasizing the *persistence* of the OOD advantage post-pruning and the methodological limitations regarding true OOD evaluation.
2.  **Terminology:** The term "generalization gap" as used in the paper (referring to the performance difference between CLIP and IN models on OOD data) is potentially confusing, as this term is widely used elsewhere (e.g., train-test gap, generalization error). Renaming this concept (e.g., "OOD performance gap" or "robustness advantage") would enhance clarity.
3.  **True OOD Experiment (Essential):** To substantiate the core claims about OOD robustness, the authors *must* conduct experiments where *entire test domains* (e.g., all sketch-style images, all cartoon-style images) are systematically identified and removed *completely* from the training data (LAION) *before* pruning. Evaluating CLIP trained on this domain-purged LAION (and potentially pruned further) on the corresponding OOD test sets is crucial for a fair assessment of true OOD generalization. This is the most critical revision needed.
4.  **Technical Correction:** Ensure consistent terminology; for example, Fig. 1 mentions "ImageNet" when comparing neighbors, but it should explicitly state "ImageNet-Train" for accuracy.

**Main Points:**
*   The paper demonstrates that pruning training images highly similar to specific test samples reduces, but does not eliminate, CLIP's OOD performance advantage over ImageNet-trained models.
*   However, the methodology does not establish a true OOD setting because it fails to remove entire test domains (e.g., all sketch images) from the training data.
*   Consequently, the persistence of CLIP's advantage could be significantly influenced by residual domain-specific information (e.g., other sketch images of different classes) remaining in the pruned training set, rather than solely reflecting robust OOD generalization capabilities. This undermines the paper's central interpretation.

**Decision:** **Accept (Marginally above acceptance threshold - Score 6)**

**Justification:** The key insight regarding the persistence of CLIP's OOD advantage despite significant reduction in image-level similarity is novel, valuable, and supported by extensive experimentation. The release of pruned datasets and code is a strong positive. However, the fundamental methodological limitation concerning the lack of true OOD evaluation (failure to remove entire domains) significantly weakens the paper's core claims about the *source* of CLIP's OOD robustness. Addressing this flaw via the suggested domain-purging experiment is essential for future work, but the current findings, resources, and the importance of the research question warrant acceptance. The paper makes a tangible, albeit qualified, contribution to understanding foundation model robustness.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.
reviews.strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?
reviews.questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: Paper [ID]

### Summary
This paper investigates the hypothesis that CLIP's strong generalization performance, particularly on out-of-distribution (OOD) benchmarks, stems significantly from high similarity between training samples in LAION-400M and test samples in these benchmarks. The authors employ a pruning strategy, removing samples from LAION-400M deemed perceptually similar to OOD benchmarks (specifically ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R) using CLIP's embedding space, and then retrain CLIP variants. The work provides compelling visual and quantitative evidence suggesting LAION contains more images similar to these OOD benchmarks than ImageNet-train, and that pruning these similar samples causes a substantial drop in CLIP's OOD accuracy. While the core idea is valuable and the initial evidence is strong, the current experimental design lacks the conclusiveness needed to fully support the central claim, and broader implications require deeper discussion.

### Strengths
1.  **Compelling Visual Evidence:** Figure 1 is highly effective. It clearly and intuitively demonstrates that LAION-400M contains numerous images semantically and stylistically similar to examples from OOD benchmarks like ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, a characteristic notably absent in ImageNet-train. This visualizes a key premise of the hypothesis.
2.  **Quantitative Similarity Analysis:** Figure 3 provides strong quantitative support. It effectively shows that the nearest neighbors of OOD benchmark images within LAION-400M are significantly more similar (in CLIP embedding space) than those within ImageNet-train (excepting ImageNet-Val, as expected). Furthermore, it demonstrates a positive correlation between the similarity of a test sample's nearest training neighbor and CLIP's accuracy on that sample, reinforcing the link between train-test similarity and performance.
3.  **Impact of Pruning:** Figure 4 presents a critical result: pruning samples from LAION-400M that are similar to a specific OOD benchmark (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch) causes a substantial drop in CLIP's accuracy *on that specific benchmark* after retraining. This provides direct experimental evidence suggesting these similar samples are crucial for CLIP's robustness on these OOD tasks.

### Weaknesses and Major Concerns
1.  **Inconclusive Pruning Results:** The results presented in Figure 4, while showing a significant drop, are ultimately inconclusive regarding the *specificity* of the effect. The core claim implies that pruning samples similar to Benchmark A should specifically harm performance *only* on Benchmark A. The paper does not demonstrate this. Crucially, it lacks experiments showing that pruning for, say, ImageNet-Sketch *does not* significantly impact performance on unrelated OOD benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet-R or others). Without this control, the performance drop could be attributed to a general degradation in model quality or robustness rather than a specific reliance on benchmark-similar samples.
2.  **Lack of Model Comparison:** A critical missing analysis is comparing CLIP's sensitivity to this pruning against a baseline model, such as a standard ImageNet-trained model. Does pruning similar samples from LAION harm CLIP's OOD generalization *more* than a comparable pruning would harm an ImageNet model's generalization? This comparison is essential to determine if CLIP is uniquely reliant on such similarities or if this is a more general phenomenon. The current work only shows the effect *within* CLIP trained on LAION.
3.  **Underdeveloped Broader Implications:** The paper touches upon fundamental concepts in zero-shot learning and OOD robustness evaluation but stops short of a meaningful discussion. The findings potentially challenge common assumptions about the "zero-shot" nature of evaluations using benchmarks like ImageNet-Sketch/R if their images are demonstrably similar to LAION training data. The paper needs to explicitly discuss the implications of its findings for: (a) how we define "zero-shot" transfer in the context of web-scale pre-training, (b) the validity and interpretation of popular OOD benchmarks when used with models trained on LAION-like datasets, and (c) what constitutes true OOD robustness versus exploitation of train-test similarity.

### Suggestions for Revision
To address the weaknesses and strengthen the paper significantly, the authors should:
1.  **Demonstrate Benchmark Specificity:** Conduct the critical experiment showing that pruning samples similar to *one specific OOD benchmark* (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch) from LAION leads to a significant accuracy drop *only on that specific benchmark*, while performance on *other, unrelated OOD benchmarks* (e.g., ImageNet-R, ObjectNet, etc.) remains largely unaffected. This is essential to isolate the effect and support the core claim of benchmark-specific reliance.
2.  **Compare Against a Baseline Model:** Perform a parallel experiment using a model trained *only* on ImageNet. Prune samples from ImageNet-train that are similar to an OOD benchmark (using the same CLIP embedding similarity method) and retrain the model. Compare the magnitude of the OOD accuracy drop on the target benchmark between the pruned CLIP model and the pruned ImageNet model. This will clarify whether CLIP's performance is *uniquely* dependent on these similar samples or if the phenomenon is more general.
3.  **Expand the Discussion:** Significantly broaden the discussion section to explicitly address the implications of the findings for:
    *   The definition and practical reality of "zero-shot learning" when pre-training datasets like LAION are used.
    *   The interpretation and potential limitations of current OOD robustness benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch, -R, -A, -V2, ObjectNet) when evaluating models trained on LAION. Does their utility change?
    *   What these findings suggest about the mechanisms underlying CLIP's robustness and the nature of robustness we should strive for and measure.

### Overall Assessment
This paper tackles an important and timely question regarding the source of CLIP's impressive generalization capabilities. The core hypothesis is well-motivated, and the initial evidence (Figures 1, 3, and 4) is visually compelling and quantitatively suggestive. The methodology of pruning based on perceptual similarity in CLIP space is appropriate. However, the current experimental design lacks the necessary controls (benchmark specificity and model comparison) to conclusively support the central claim that CLIP's OOD robustness *specifically* stems from exploiting benchmark-similar training data. Furthermore, the significant broader implications for zero-shot learning definitions and OOD benchmark usage are not adequately explored. Addressing these weaknesses through the suggested revisions is crucial for establishing the paper's conclusions and impact.

**Decision: Reject (Marginally Below Acceptance Threshold)**
While the work presents valuable insights and a promising approach, the lack of conclusive evidence regarding the specificity of the pruning effect and the absence of a comparative analysis with other models, combined with the underdeveloped discussion of broader implications, currently prevent it from meeting the acceptance threshold. The suggested revisions are substantial but necessary to elevate the paper to the required level of rigor and impact. I encourage the authors to address these points and consider resubmission.

The review highlights strong visual and quantitative evidence but notes weaknesses in conclusiveness and suggests broader discussion on zero-shot learning and OOD robustness.
strengths: Figure 1 clearly shows LAION-400M contains semantically/stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks unlike ImageNet-train

Figure 3 quantitatively demonstrates nearest neighbors of OOD datasets are more similar in LAION-400M than ImageNet-train (except ImageNet-Val) and shows CLIP performs better on samples with similar training images
Figure 4 shows pruning similar samples to OOD benchmarks causes substantial accuracy drop, indicating these samples are crucial for CLIP's robustness

title: Simple Hierarchical Planning with Diffusion

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.
reviews.strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.
reviews.weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.
reviews.questions: See weakness above.

Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.

Strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.

Weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Hierarchical Diffuser: Two-Level Diffusion Planning with Sub-Goals"

**Summary of Contribution:**  
This paper introduces the Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a novel two-level hierarchical planning framework that decomposes long-horizon tasks into sub-goals using diffusion models. HD employs a high-level "sparse diffuser" to generate sub-goals and a low-level "standard diffuser" to plan state-action sequences between them. Evaluations across Maze2D, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym, and FrankaKitchen environments demonstrate improved performance, out-of-distribution generalization, and computational efficiency compared to the base Diffuser and hierarchical RL baselines. While the empirical results are compelling, concerns regarding technical novelty and architectural limitations temper the contribution’s significance.

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Effective Hierarchical Decomposition:** Discretizing planning via diffusion-generated sub-goals enables handling more diverse and complex scenarios than flat diffusion approaches.  
2.  **Task-Agnostic Low-Level Planning:** The separation allows the low-level diffuser to operate independently of high-level task specifics, enhancing flexibility.  
3.  **Superior Receptive Field via Sparse Diffuser (SD):** The proposed SD component outperforms the original Diffuser, primarily due to its increased receptive field capturing longer-range dependencies.  
4.  **Addressing Hierarchical RL Bottlenecks:** SD’s use of dense actions effectively fits sparse objectives, mitigating a known limitation in hierarchical RL.  
5.  **Computational Efficiency:** HD significantly reduces planning time by shortening high-level sequences and enabling parallel low-level trajectory generation between sub-goals.

**Weaknesses and Concerns:**  
1.  **Limited Novelty:** The core formulation combines two existing diffuser types (sparse and standard) in a hierarchical manner without introducing fundamentally new technical mechanisms. The conceptual leap appears incremental.  
2.  **Sub-Goal Independence Risk:** Optimizing sub-goals independently, without explicit coordination, risks non-optimal behavior and potential path overlaps or conflicts between adjacent segments.  
3.  **Lack of Feedback Mechanism:** A critical omission is the absence of feedback from the low-level to the high-level diffuser. If low-level actions fail to reach a sub-goal, there is no mechanism for the high-level planner to adapt, potentially leading to compounding errors.  
4.  **Ambiguity in Sampling:** The paper lacks clarity on the number of trajectories sampled per segment/sub-goal during planning, making reproducibility and understanding of stochastic behavior difficult.  
5.  **Magnitude of Contribution:** While HD demonstrates empirical improvements, the combination of limited novelty and the identified architectural weaknesses suggests the overall contribution is not sufficiently significant for a high-impact publication.

**Technical Evaluation:**  
The experimental design is sound, benchmarking against relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL methods. The ablation study showing that increasing kernel size in Diffuser improves performance at the cost of generalization is insightful. HD’s key advantage lies in avoiding this trade-off, achieving both better performance and generalization. However, the technical core relies on established diffusion planning techniques arranged hierarchically.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1.  **Ablation on State-Action Modeling:** To better justify the choice of modeling state-action sequences over states alone (e.g., as in Decision Diffuser), include an ablation study comparing HD against a variant using state-only diffusion for the low-level planner. This would strengthen the rationale for the current design choice.

**Overall Assessment:**  
The Hierarchical Diffuser presents a well-executed application of hierarchical decomposition to diffusion-based planning, yielding demonstrable gains in performance, generalization, and efficiency over the base Diffuser. The empirical results across diverse benchmarks are a strength. However, the work is hampered by concerns regarding the novelty of its core formulation and significant architectural limitations, particularly the independent optimization of sub-goals and the lack of a feedback loop from low-level to high-level planning. These weaknesses limit the perceived significance of the contribution.  

**Decision:**  
I recommend **acceptance** as the paper marginally exceeds the acceptance threshold. The empirical improvements and the practical solution to long-horizon diffusion planning are valuable, but the concerns noted above prevent a higher rating. Addressing the feedback mechanism and clarifying the sampling strategy in a revision would strengthen the work.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes an hierarchical variant of Diffuser. The algorithm involves using a high-level planner that plans trajectories in a sparser manner and a low-level planner that adds detailed states to the trajectories produces by the high-level planner. Experiment result demonstrate that hierarchical planning produces better performance in long-horizon planning tasks and offline RL tasks. The authers also study the impact of kernel size in Diffuser and the impact of the jumpy steps. An interesting observation is that Diffuser with a small receptive field can not cover the data distribution well.
reviews.strengths:
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The method is straightforward and demonstrates strong results in long-horizon planning tasks, especially the AntMaze.
3. The study of relationship between receptive field of the diffusion model and the data coverage ratio is interesting and offers valuable insight for futher research on diffusion models for planning in more complex domains.
reviews.weaknesses:
One minor weakness is that the baseline Decision Diffuser[1] is missing. Also the study presented in this paper focuses on using the CNN architecture as Diffuser does. A comparison between using CNN and transformer architecutre, such as Decision Diffuser, would be encouraged.

[1] Ajay, Anurag, et al. "Is conditional generative modeling all you need for decision-making?." ICLR 2023.
reviews.questions:
1. According to Fig 3., Diffuser with a small kernel size covers one mode of the data distribution during actual execution. Is it the case that the standard Diffuser is able to \textbf{cover} the full data distribution but just fails to achieve full data coverage during actual execution? If so, what might be the cause the such gap?
2. Still concerning Fig 3., although HD seems to cover most of the data distribution, HD fails to cover two short segments/branches. Why does such pheomena occur?

reviews.text:
## Peer Review

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a hierarchical variant of the Diffuser model, featuring a high-level planner for sparse trajectory generation and a low-level planner for detail refinement. The approach demonstrates performance improvements in long-horizon planning tasks, particularly within AntMaze environments. A significant contribution is the empirical analysis exploring the relationship between kernel size, receptive fields in diffusion models, and their impact on data coverage during execution.

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Clarity and Presentation:** The paper is well-written and clearly structured, making the proposed method and its contributions accessible to the reader.
2.  **Effective Method & Strong Results:** The hierarchical planning approach is conceptually straightforward and yields strong empirical results, particularly in challenging long-horizon tasks like AntMaze, demonstrating its practical value.
3.  **Valuable Analysis:** The analysis investigating how the receptive field size in diffusion models affects data coverage is a significant and insightful contribution. This empirical demonstration of the limitations imposed by small receptive fields is particularly valuable for the field.

**Weaknesses:**  
1.  **Missing Baseline Comparison:** A notable omission is the lack of comparison against the Decision Diffuser baseline. Including this comparison is crucial for accurately positioning the performance gains offered by the proposed hierarchical approach.
2.  **Limited Architecture Analysis:** While the method is presented, the paper does not provide a comparative analysis between the potential implementations using CNN versus transformer architectures for the planners. Exploring the trade-offs or performance differences between these choices would strengthen the technical discussion.

**Main Contributions:**  
1.  The introduction of hierarchical planning (HDiffuser) effectively addresses long-horizon planning challenges, as evidenced by improved performance in tasks like AntMaze.
2.  The empirical demonstration of the critical relationship between kernel size/receptive field and the model's ability to cover the full data distribution during execution. This highlights a key limitation of standard diffusers with small receptive fields.
3.  The finding that diffusers with small receptive fields, while theoretically capable of covering the full distribution, fail to do so in practice during the execution phase, necessitating hierarchical approaches or larger kernels.

**Questions & Areas for Clarification:**  
1.  The paper identifies that standard diffusers with small receptive fields *theoretically* cover the full data distribution but fail during *execution*. What is the hypothesized cause for this gap between theoretical capability and practical failure? Further discussion on the underlying reasons (e.g., optimization challenges, mode collapse exacerbated by autoregressive rollout) would be beneficial.
2.  In Figure 3, HDiffuser is shown to successfully cover long branches but fails to cover two short branches. What is the explanation for this specific failure mode? Understanding why the hierarchical approach struggles with these shorter trajectories despite its overall success with longer horizons would provide deeper insight into its limitations.

**Recommendation:**  
This paper presents a well-motivated hierarchical approach to diffusion planning that yields strong empirical results in long-horizon tasks. The analysis of kernel size and receptive field limitations provides a valuable and insightful contribution to understanding diffusion models for planning. While the core contributions are solid, the lack of comparison to the Decision Diffuser baseline and a deeper dive into architectural choices are weaknesses that slightly diminish the paper's impact. Addressing the raised questions regarding the theoretical/execution gap and the specific failure in Figure 3 would also strengthen the work. **Overall, the strengths, particularly the novel hierarchical method and the significant coverage analysis, outweigh the weaknesses. The paper meets the acceptance threshold and is recommended for acceptance as is, though addressing the points above would further enhance its contribution.**

**Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary: The authors of this paper present Hierarchical Diffuser, a simple yet effective hierarchical planning approach using diffusion models to improve the performance and efficiency of diffusion-based planning for long-horizon tasks. By utilizing a two-pronged structure, the Hierarchical Diffuser combines a high-level planner, the Sparse Diffuser, to generate sub-goals, and a low-level planner to refine the plan. This approach significantly outperforms non-hierarchical planning methods, while also enhancing computational efficiency in training and planning speed. Furthermore, the method displays improved generalization capabilities on out-of-distribution tasks compared to non-hierarchical Diffusers.
reviews.strengths:
- Originality: This work creatively combines the strengths of hierarchical planning and diffusion models, achieving significant improvements in performance and computational efficiency compared to existing methods.
- Quality: The contribution provides a thorough theoretical analysis of the method, backed by well-designed experiments and in-depth analyses. The authors emphasize the rationale behind the design decisions and the resultant benefits of their approach.
- Clarity: The paper is well-organized, clearly written, and explains the method along with its advantages and limitations in a coherent and accessible way.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Considering the existence of the HDMI algorithm, the contribution of this paper are limited. However, compared to HDMI, this paper has conducted extensive experiments on generalization and provided corresponding theoretical support. One suggestion I have is that the authors can emphasize the advantages of Hierarchical Diffuser from the perspective of generalization, and highlight the algorithm's generalization performance in the experimental design.
- In the hierarchical framework, the quality of the generated high-level goals is crucial to the effectiveness of the algorithm. The authors emphasize that the Hierarchical Diffuser is simpler compared to existing hierarchical methods but have only designed one goal selection method. In addition to the fixed time interval heuristic method used in this paper, HDMI also mentions two heuristic methods based on spatial intervals and reward scale, which are equally simple and have low implementation costs. If the authors could take these heuristic methods into consideration, I believe it would have a positive impact on the completeness of the paper's content. Furthermore, the demonstration of the quality of the generated goals is essential, and there is a gap in this aspect in the current paper.
reviews.questions:
- I have noticed that both Diffuser and Decision Diffuser employ “inpainting” techniques for trajectory generation in goal-conditioned tasks and the lower-level planner of HDMI. However, this paper utilizes return-guided sampling. What were the considerations behind the authors' choice between the two methods?
- This paper proposes to get the sub-goals first with the planning-based subgoal extractor and then train HDMI with the sub-goals as supervision. Although this two-step process makes sense, the overall training process becomes ad hoc and less general. Is there any connection between the upper and lower layers of Hierarchical Diffuser's planner? Can it be trained end-to-end?
