reviews.summary: The authors develop a theoretical framework for understand why offline reactivation occurs in recurrent neural networks. In particular, the authors argue that neural noise during awake states is essential for the emergence of faithful and varied reactivation of neural trajectories during quiscience (i.e. in absence of external stimuli).
reviews.strengths:
- the paper is generally well written
- the mathematical theory is well presented and interesting (as far as I'm aware it is novel, but I am not certain of this)
- offline reactivation should be of interest both to machine learning and neuroscience researchers; the paper does make some theoretical and experimental headway into why/how it occurs
reviews.weaknesses:
- perhaps this is not in the scope of the paper, but the authors do not provide any theoretical/numerical support for the functional benefit of offline reactivation. The authors mention other works which demonstrate that it may aid the formation of long-term memories, schema, planning. Do the theoretical/numerical results in this paper support these possibilities, or any one in particular?
- Relatedly, a key feature of this study is that the RNNs considered are noisy during awake states. It's unclear to me exactly why this is necessary. The authors "found that even trajectories generated by networks that were not trained in the presence of noise, and also were not driven by noise in the quiescent phase, still generated quiescent activity distributions that corresponded well to the active phase distributions"; is the point that there's more space exploration with noise, giving rise to a functional benefit? Or that somehow the introduction of noise better captures observed neural data?
- I found the link between the theoretically optimal solution for update dynamics and the actual application of RNNs somewhat unclear. Are we to take that (presumably backprop-trained) RNNs employ update dynamics similarly to the theoretical solution; is it biologically reasonable for e.g. equation 18 to be implement by an RNN? If so, is it possible to show that they do; e.g. by comparing these recurrent inputs to the optimal solution? If not, are the experimental results relevant to the theory? 
- Relatedly, perhaps this is overly harsh, but I also do not find the result that RNNs without stimuli visit similar states as with stimuli very surprising.
- The authors suggest an interesting discrepancy in experimental prediction for their noise-based theory of offline reactivation and generative modeling; specifically, that "while generative models necessarily recapitulate the moment-to-moment transition statistics of sensory data, our approach only predicts that the stationary distribution will be identical". I am confused by this statement and not sure if it is true. Is that to say that the proposed model would not encode sensory transitions but would simply replicate the overall probability distribution of sensory states? I would find this surprising for an RNN whose recurrent weights are trained on sensory data; that is, I would suppose that the RNN weights would themselves somehow capture transition statistics of the task variables (e.g. maybe this is valuable as a kind of denoising effect on noisy observations). Perhaps I am wrong though
reviews.questions:
- In section 2.1 it may be relatively obvious that p denotes probability, but I would still clarify this
- In equation 15 it's a bit odd to provide a new definition for L_{noise} having just defined it previousply with the D term involved
- the delta t is given as 0.02. What's the unit, seconds?
- I would recommend a limitations section
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors develop a theoretical framework for understand why offline reactivation occurs in recurrent neural networks. In particular, the authors argue that neural noise during awake states is essential for the emergence of faithful and varied reactivation of neural trajectories during quiscience (i.e. in absence of external stimuli).

Strengths:
- the paper is generally well written
- the mathematical theory is well presented and interesting (as far as I'm aware it is novel, but I am not certain of this)
- offline reactivation should be of interest both to machine learning and neuroscience researchers; the paper does make some theoretical and experimental headway into why/how it occurs

Weaknesses:
- perhaps this is not in the scope of the paper, but the authors do not provide any theoretical/numerical support for the functional benefit of offline reactivation. The authors mention other works which demonstrate that it may aid the formation of long-term memories, schema, planning. Do the theoretical/numerical results in this paper support these possibilities, or any one in particular?
- Relatedly, a key feature of this study is that the RNNs considered are noisy during awake states. It's unclear to me exactly why this is necessary. The authors "found that even trajectories generated by networks that were not trained in the presence of noise, and also were not driven by noise in the quiescent phase, still generated quiescent activity distributions that corresponded well to the active phase distributions"; is the point that there's more space exploration with noise, giving rise to a functional benefit? Or that somehow the introduction of noise better captures observed neural data?
- I found the link between the theoretically optimal solution for update dynamics and the actual application of RNNs somewhat unclear. Are we to take that (presumably backprop-trained) RNNs employ update dynamics similarly to the theoretical solution; is it biologically reasonable for e.g. equation 18 to be implement by an RNN? If so, is it possible to show that they do; e.g. by comparing these recurrent inputs to the optimal solution? If not, are the experimental results relevant to the theory? 
- Relatedly, perhaps this is overly harsh, but I also do not find the result that RNNs without stimuli visit similar states as with stimuli very surprising.
- The authors suggest an interesting discrepancy in experimental prediction for their noise-based theory of offline reactivation and generative modeling; specifically, that "while generative models necessarily recapitulate the moment-to-moment transition statistics of sensory data, our approach only predicts that the stationary distribution will be identical". I am confused by this statement and not sure if it is true. Is that to say that the proposed model would not encode sensory transitions but would simply replicate the overall probability distribution of sensory states? I would find this surprising for an RNN whose recurrent weights are trained on sensory data; that is, I would suppose that the RNN weights would themselves somehow capture transition statistics of the task variables (e.g. maybe this is valuable as a kind of denoising effect on noisy observations). Perhaps I am wrong though

Questions:
- In section 2.1 it may be relatively obvious that p denotes probability, but I would still clarify this
- In equation 15 it's a bit odd to provide a new definition for L_{noise} having just defined it previousply with the D term involved
- the delta t is given as 0.02. What's the unit, seconds?
- I would recommend a limitations section

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: Theoretical Framework for Offline Reactivation in RNNs  

**Summary of Review:**  
This manuscript presents a theoretical framework exploring how neural noise during awake states facilitates faithful offline reactivation in recurrent neural networks (RNNs). The work addresses a topic of significant relevance to both computational neuroscience and machine learning communities. While the mathematical theory is well-presented and the paper is clearly written, several conceptual and technical concerns weaken the impact. These include insufficient justification for the functional role of noise, limited connection to biological/cognitive benefits, and gaps in aligning theoretical predictions with empirical RNN behavior. Despite these limitations, the core ideas are sufficiently interesting to warrant publication with major revisions.  

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Clarity and Presentation:** The paper is generally well-written, with a logical flow and accessible explanations of complex concepts.  
2.  **Mathematical Framework:** The development of the mathematical theory (relating awake-state noise to offline reactivation) is interesting and well-presented, offering a potentially novel perspective.  
3.  **Relevance:** The investigation of offline reactivation mechanisms bridges fundamental questions in neuroscience (e.g., memory replay) and machine learning (training RNN dynamics), appealing to a broad interdisciplinary audience.  

**Weaknesses and Major Concerns:**  
1.  **Functional Significance Unexplored:** The paper lacks a substantial discussion on the *functional benefits* of the proposed offline reactivation enabled by awake-state noise. How does this process contribute to memory consolidation, planning, or generalization in biological or artificial systems? Addressing this is crucial for impact.  
2.  **Necessity of Noise Unjustified:** The core hypothesis—that noise *during the awake state* is necessary for faithful offline reactivation—requires stronger justification. Why couldn't other mechanisms (e.g., specific network architectures, training protocols) achieve similar reactivation without relying on this specific form of noise? The experimental results presented (RNNs revisiting states without stimuli) are not particularly surprising on their own and do not convincingly demonstrate the unique necessity of awake noise.  
3.  **Theory-Implementation Gap:** The link between the derived optimal update dynamics (based on the theoretical loss function) and the dynamics actually implemented by standardly trained RNNs (e.g., via backpropagation through time) is weak. The paper needs to clarify whether and how commonly used RNN training procedures lead to networks that approximate the theoretically optimal dynamics proposed here.  
4.  **Discrepancy in Predictions:** There is a concerning discrepancy between the theory's key prediction (that the stationary distribution during reactivation matches the awake distribution) and the focus of many generative models on learning *transition statistics* (e.g., in spatial navigation). The manuscript should explicitly address this apparent conflict and discuss its implications.  

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
*   **Add a Dedicated Limitations Section:** This section should explicitly address the gaps identified above: the lack of discussion on functional benefits, the weak justification for the necessity of awake-state noise, the theory-implementation gap, and the discrepancy between stationary distribution predictions and transition-based models.  
*   **Strengthen Justification for Noise:** Provide stronger theoretical arguments or additional computational experiments specifically designed to test the *necessity* of awake-state noise for faithful reactivation, contrasting it with alternative mechanisms.  
*   **Clarify Theory-Implementation Link:** Discuss more explicitly how the derived optimal dynamics relate to the training and dynamics of practical RNN implementations. Does standard training implicitly encourage dynamics that minimize the proposed loss functions?  
*   **Address Functional Role:** Expand the discussion to hypothesize or reference potential cognitive or computational functions enabled by the proposed reactivation mechanism (e.g., memory consolidation, model-based planning).  

**Technical Corrections:**  
1.  **Probability Notation (Sec 2.1):** Clarify the meaning of the probability notation `p` (e.g., `p(x)`). Is it a probability mass function (PMF), probability density function (PDF), or a general measure? Define it explicitly.  
2.  **Equation 15 (L_noise):** Address the apparent inconsistency in the definition of `L_noise`. The text mentions it depends on `x_{t+Δt}`, but the equation does not reflect this clearly. Ensure the equation aligns with the textual description.  
3.  **Units for Δt:** Specify the units for the timestep `Δt = 0.02` used in the simulations (e.g., seconds, arbitrary units).  

**Overall Assessment:**  
The manuscript introduces an interesting theoretical framework with clear potential relevance. The mathematical development is a strength, and the core question of offline reactivation is important. However, the weaknesses, particularly the lack of discussion on functional benefits and insufficient justification for the necessity and role of awake-state noise, significantly limit the paper's current impact. Furthermore, the link between the derived optimal dynamics and standard RNN training/behavior needs clarification. Addressing the major concerns outlined above, particularly through a comprehensive limitations section and strengthened justification, is essential.  

**Decision:**  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.** I recommend **acceptance pending major revisions**. The revisions should comprehensively address the concerns regarding functional significance, noise necessity, theory-implementation alignment, and the technical points listed. The addition of a substantive limitations section is crucial. The current merits of the theory and presentation are sufficient for acceptance, but the required revisions are substantial and necessary for the paper to realize its potential impact.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors develop a theoretical framework for understand why offline reactivation occurs in recurrent neural networks. In particular, the authors argue that neural noise during awake states is essential for the emergence of faithful and varied reactivation of neural trajectories during quiscience (i.e. in absence of external stimuli).

Strengths:
- the paper is generally well written
- the mathematical theory is well presented and interesting (as far as I'm aware it is novel, but I am not certain of this)
- offline reactivation should be of interest both to machine learning and neuroscience researchers; the paper does make some theoretical and experimental headway into why/how it occurs

Weaknesses:
- perhaps this is not in the scope of the paper, but the authors do not provide any theoretical/numerical support for the functional benefit of offline reactivation. The authors mention other works which demonstrate that it may aid the formation of long-term memories, schema, planning. Do the theoretical/numerical results in this paper support these possibilities, or any one in particular?
- Relatedly, a key feature of this study is that the RNNs considered are noisy during awake states. It's unclear to me exactly why this is necessary. The authors "found that even trajectories generated by networks that were not trained in the presence of noise, and also were not driven by noise in the quiescent phase, still generated quiescent activity distributions that corresponded well to the active phase distributions"; is the point that there's more space exploration with noise, giving rise to a functional benefit? Or that somehow the introduction of noise better captures observed neural data?
- I found the link between the theoretically optimal solution for update dynamics and the actual application of RNNs somewhat unclear. Are we to take that (presumably backprop-trained) RNNs employ update dynamics similarly to the theoretical solution; is it biologically reasonable for e.g. equation 18 to be implement by an RNN? If so, is it possible to show that they do; e.g. by comparing these recurrent inputs to the optimal solution? If not, are the experimental results relevant to the theory? 
- Relatedly, perhaps this is overly harsh, but I also do not find the result that RNNs without stimuli visit similar states as with stimuli very surprising.
- The authors suggest an interesting discrepancy in experimental prediction for their noise-based theory of offline reactivation and generative modeling; specifically, that "while generative models necessarily recapitulate the moment-to-moment transition statistics of sensory data, our approach only predicts that the stationary distribution will be identical". I am confused by this statement and not sure if it is true. Is that to say that the proposed model would not encode sensory transitions but would simply replicate the overall probability distribution of sensory states? I would find this surprising for an RNN whose recurrent weights are trained on sensory data; that is, I would suppose that the RNN weights would themselves somehow capture transition statistics of the task variables (e.g. maybe this is valuable as a kind of denoising effect on noisy observations). Perhaps I am wrong though

Questions:
- In section 2.1 it may be relatively obvious that p denotes probability, but I would still clarify this
- In equation 15 it's a bit odd to provide a new definition for L_{noise} having just defined it previousply with the D term involved
- the delta t is given as 0.02. What's the unit, seconds?
- I would recommend a limitations section

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The authors demonstrate that training network dynamics on an upper bound of the cost function for learning a function of an input stimulus in the presence of network noise results in network dynamics that separate into a denoising component and a signal integration component.  Furthermore, they provide conditions such that, in the absence	of input, the network will reproduce typical states of the (learned function of the) input stimulus.  Notably, this strictly requires the input noise to be different in the quiescent vs input-driven cases.  The authors demonstrate numerical simulations in which networks reflect these results.
reviews.strengths: This paper does a nice job of outlining how the cost function can be deconstructed into two components, the denoising component and the signal integration component.  The idea of replay being a result of signal integration in the presence of noise is an interesting one, and the authors present a simple mechanism whereby this can arise. This is a contribution to an important question in neuroscience.  The theoretical approach, results, and the numerical experiments are well described and supported.
reviews.weaknesses:
The paper is potentially confusing to some readers in that the presentation (and the numerical experiments if I have followed) regard training a network with a fixed nonlinearity and trainable weight parameters (i.e. a typical RNN) but the theoretical analysis considers not a fixed nonlinearity, but rather the space of all possible dynamics that could govern the system.  I suspect this may misdirect some readers regarding the basic argument (possibly me as well).  

Training a regular RNN on the cost function, L, of course, does not guarantee anything about the cost function L_upper, so one must make some additional assumptions about the structure of the network dynamics, namely something akin to demanding that the space of permissible dynamics admits the derived minimum of L_upper.  In the case of the numerical experiments, the authors use ReLU and decompose the input to the nonlinearity into a recurrent part and a signal integration part, which should approximately allow for the decomposition that leads to L_upper.  One needs to add an argument that this decomposition is reasonable for certain classes of RNNs. E.g. If the network is in the active regime, the ReLU doesn't contribute much and then the composition amounts to considering the activity updates from the recurrent part and the input part separately.  One could make a similar argument about sigmoids.  In short, the authors should, I think, be direct about the assumptions on the RNN necessary for their theory. 

Regarding the quiescent state, I think the authors are perhaps a little too cavalier with the fact that, strictly speaking, the noise term must change in order to guarantee replay.  As presented at the moment, it feels to this reader like a weakness that is not adequately addressed.  The authors should either supply 1) a reason to believe this sort of change happens in real networks or 2) a demonstration that the deviations are not sufficient to qualitatively change the main result.  There is also the possibility that this is an opportunity for the authors.  The deviations from perfect replay if the noise does not change amount to a prediction, and one which may have computational advantages (e.g. facilitating exploration in terms of a planning algorithm).
reviews.questions:
Last sentence of the introduction:  I think "ecologically" should be "ethnologically"

Questions:

- Does this work for more general RNNs (more general nonlinearities, interactions between recurrence and signal integration, etc.)?

- Is there reason to believe the noise distribution changes in the quiescent state?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors demonstrate that training network dynamics on an upper bound of the cost function for learning a function of an input stimulus in the presence of network noise results in network dynamics that separate into a denoising component and a signal integration component.  Furthermore, they provide conditions such that, in the absence	of input, the network will reproduce typical states of the (learned function of the) input stimulus.  Notably, this strictly requires the input noise to be different in the quiescent vs input-driven cases.  The authors demonstrate numerical simulations in which networks reflect these results.

Strengths:
This paper does a nice job of outlining how the cost function can be deconstructed into two components, the denoising component and the signal integration component.  The idea of replay being a result of signal integration in the presence of noise is an interesting one, and the authors present a simple mechanism whereby this can arise. This is a contribution to an important question in neuroscience.  The theoretical approach, results, and the numerical experiments are well described and supported.

Weaknesses:
The paper is potentially confusing to some readers in that the presentation (and the numerical experiments if I have followed) regard training a network with a fixed nonlinearity and trainable weight parameters (i.e. a typical RNN) but the theoretical analysis considers not a fixed nonlinearity, but rather the space of all possible dynamics that could govern the system.  I suspect this may misdirect some readers regarding the basic argument (possibly me as well).  

Training a regular RNN on the cost function, L, of course, does not guarantee anything about the cost function L_upper, so one must make some additional assumptions about the structure of the network dynamics, namely something akin to demanding that the space of permissible dynamics admits the derived minimum of L_upper.  In the case of the numerical experiments, the authors use ReLU and decompose the input to the nonlinearity into a recurrent part and a signal integration part, which should approximately allow for the decomposition that leads to L_upper.  One needs to add an argument that this decomposition is reasonable for certain classes of RNNs. E.g. If the network is in the active regime, the ReLU doesn't contribute much and then the composition amounts to considering the activity updates from the recurrent part and the input part separately.  One could make a similar argument about sigmoids.  In short, the authors should, I think, be direct about the assumptions on the RNN necessary for their theory. 

Regarding the quiescent state, I think the authors are perhaps a little too cavalier with the fact that, strictly speaking, the noise term must change in order to guarantee replay.  As presented at the moment, it feels to this reader like a weakness that is not adequately addressed.  The authors should either supply 1) a reason to believe this sort of change happens in real networks or 2) a demonstration that the deviations are not sufficient to qualitatively change the main result.  There is also the possibility that this is an opportunity for the authors.  The deviations from perfect replay if the noise does not change amount to a prediction, and one which may have computational advantages (e.g. facilitating exploration in terms of a planning algorithm).

Questions:
Last sentence of the introduction:  I think "ecologically" should be "ethnologically"

Questions:

- Does this work for more general RNNs (more general nonlinearities, interactions between recurrence and signal integration, etc.)?

- Is there reason to believe the noise distribution changes in the quiescent state?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Neural Reactivation through Denoising and Signal Integration Dynamics"

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a theoretically grounded exploration of how neural reactivation (replay) emerges from denoising dynamics in optimally trained recurrent neural networks (RNNs). The core contribution lies in deriving mathematical conditions under which reactivation is guaranteed, decomposing the cost function into denoising and signal integration components. Numerical experiments on navigation and head-direction tasks provide validation. While the theoretical framework is elegant and addresses a significant neuroscience question, concerns arise regarding the alignment between the theoretical assumptions and the practical implementations, particularly concerning noise requirements during quiescent states. These concerns temper the impact of an otherwise compelling contribution.

**Major Comments (Strengths and Weaknesses):**

**Strengths:**
1.  **Theoretical Clarity:** The decomposition of the cost function into distinct denoising and signal integration components is clearly outlined and forms a solid foundation for the theoretical analysis.
2.  **Novel Mechanism:** The paper proposes an interesting and potentially important mechanism linking neural replay to signal integration within inherently noisy neural systems.
3.  **Significant Question:** The work tackles a fundamental and highly relevant question in computational neuroscience regarding the emergence and function of replay.
4.  **Presentation:** The theoretical approach, results, and supporting numerical experiments are generally well-described and appear technically sound within their defined scope.

**Weaknesses and Key Concerns:**
1.  **Scope Mismatch (Theory vs. Experiment):** There is a potential source of confusion for the reader regarding the scope of the theoretical claims versus the numerical validation. The theoretical analysis claims generality over "all possible dynamics," but the numerical experiments are conducted using specific RNN architectures (fixed-nonlinearity RNNs, particularly ReLU-based with decomposed recurrent/signal inputs). The paper needs to more explicitly clarify the relationship between the broad theoretical space and the narrower practical implementations, and address the generalizability to RNNs with different nonlinearities (e.g., sigmoid) or less decomposed recurrent-signal interactions.
2.  **Training Objective Guarantee:** A critical theoretical gap exists. The paper demonstrates that minimizing the derived upper bound loss, `L_upper`, guarantees the desired denoising dynamics. However, training the network on the original task loss `L` does not automatically ensure minimization of `L_upper` without additional assumptions about the network's structure or properties. The paper needs to explicitly state the required assumptions (e.g., specific decomposability properties inherent to ReLU or sigmoid networks) that bridge this gap and justify why training on `L` would lead to minimization of `L_upper` in the implemented architectures.
3.  **Noise Variance Requirement - Biological Plausibility and Justification:** The theoretical guarantee of reactivation relies on a very specific and stringent condition: the noise variance must *double* during quiescent states compared to active states. The paper provides inadequate justification for the biological plausibility of such a precise, large-magnitude change in noise distribution between brain states. This requirement is a significant limitation. The authors must either:
    *   Provide stronger biological evidence or justification for this specific noise modulation,
    *   Demonstrate through analysis or simulation that the core results are robust to deviations from this exact factor (e.g., showing reactivation occurs over a range of increased noise levels),
    *   Or, clearly frame this specific doubling requirement as a testable experimental prediction derived from the theory, rather than a biologically established fact.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**
1.  Explicitly state the assumptions about the RNN architecture (e.g., decomposable dynamics facilitated by ReLU/sigmoid nonlinearities and input structure) that are necessary to ensure that training on the standard loss `L` effectively minimizes the theoretical upper bound `L_upper`.
2.  Significantly address the noise variance requirement concern (Weakness #3). Choose one or more of the paths suggested above (biological justification, robustness analysis, framing as a prediction) and integrate this discussion prominently.
3.  Provide a clearer discussion upfront and in the results/discussion sections delineating the scope of the theoretical analysis ("all possible dynamics") versus the scope of the numerical validation (specific RNN implementations). Discuss the implications of this difference and the potential limitations regarding generalizability to other network types.

**Minor Comments:**
1.  **Technical Detail - Noise Factor:** Ensure the critical requirement that noise variance must increase by a factor of 2 in quiescent states for the theoretical guarantees to hold is stated clearly and consistently.
2.  **Technical Detail - RNN Implementation:** The description of the numerical implementations using ReLU networks with decomposed recurrent and signal inputs is adequate, but the limitations this imposes (as per Major Comment #1) need highlighting.
3.  **Terminology:** In the introduction, the term "ecologically" (e.g., "ecologically relevant tasks") is likely intended to be "ethologically." Please correct this.

**Overall Assessment:**  
This paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by formally deriving conditions under which neural reactivation emerges as a consequence of denoising dynamics in trained RNNs, linking it to signal integration. The theoretical framework is well-constructed, and the numerical experiments on concrete tasks provide supportive evidence. However, the concerns regarding the potential mismatch between the theoretical generality and the specific numerical implementations, the lack of explicit justification for how training on `L` minimizes `L_upper`, and particularly the biologically questionable requirement for a precise doubling of noise variance during quiescence, limit the paper's immediate impact and broader applicability. Addressing these concerns, especially regarding the noise modulation, is crucial for strengthening the biological relevance and theoretical robustness of the proposed mechanism.

**Recommendation:**  
**Marginally above the acceptance threshold (Rating: 6)**. The theoretical insight is significant, but the current limitations, especially concerning the noise variance requirement and the theory-implementation scope, prevent a stronger endorsement. Acceptance is recommended contingent on the authors adequately addressing the major concerns outlined above, particularly providing a convincing justification or framing for the noise modulation requirement and clarifying the relationship between theory and implementation.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors demonstrate that training network dynamics on an upper bound of the cost function for learning a function of an input stimulus in the presence of network noise results in network dynamics that separate into a denoising component and a signal integration component.  Furthermore, they provide conditions such that, in the absence	of input, the network will reproduce typical states of the (learned function of the) input stimulus.  Notably, this strictly requires the input noise to be different in the quiescent vs input-driven cases.  The authors demonstrate numerical simulations in which networks reflect these results.

Strengths:
This paper does a nice job of outlining how the cost function can be deconstructed into two components, the denoising component and the signal integration component.  The idea of replay being a result of signal integration in the presence of noise is an interesting one, and the authors present a simple mechanism whereby this can arise. This is a contribution to an important question in neuroscience.  The theoretical approach, results, and the numerical experiments are well described and supported.

Weaknesses:
The paper is potentially confusing to some readers in that the presentation (and the numerical experiments if I have followed) regard training a network with a fixed nonlinearity and trainable weight parameters (i.e. a typical RNN) but the theoretical analysis considers not a fixed nonlinearity, but rather the space of all possible dynamics that could govern the system.  I suspect this may misdirect some readers regarding the basic argument (possibly me as well).  

Training a regular RNN on the cost function, L, of course, does not guarantee anything about the cost function L_upper, so one must make some additional assumptions about the structure of the network dynamics, namely something akin to demanding that the space of permissible dynamics admits the derived minimum of L_upper.  In the case of the numerical experiments, the authors use ReLU and decompose the input to the nonlinearity into a recurrent part and a signal integration part, which should approximately allow for the decomposition that leads to L_upper.  One needs to add an argument that this decomposition is reasonable for certain classes of RNNs. E.g. If the network is in the active regime, the ReLU doesn't contribute much and then the composition amounts to considering the activity updates from the recurrent part and the input part separately.  One could make a similar argument about sigmoids.  In short, the authors should, I think, be direct about the assumptions on the RNN necessary for their theory. 

Regarding the quiescent state, I think the authors are perhaps a little too cavalier with the fact that, strictly speaking, the noise term must change in order to guarantee replay.  As presented at the moment, it feels to this reader like a weakness that is not adequately addressed.  The authors should either supply 1) a reason to believe this sort of change happens in real networks or 2) a demonstration that the deviations are not sufficient to qualitatively change the main result.  There is also the possibility that this is an opportunity for the authors.  The deviations from perfect replay if the noise does not change amount to a prediction, and one which may have computational advantages (e.g. facilitating exploration in terms of a planning algorithm).

Questions:
Last sentence of the introduction:  I think "ecologically" should be "ethnologically"

Questions:

- Does this work for more general RNNs (more general nonlinearities, interactions between recurrence and signal integration, etc.)?

- Is there reason to believe the noise distribution changes in the quiescent state?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary:
This submission seeks to model the reactivation of brain activity during periods of quiescence using task-optimized network. The paper develops some mathematical formalisms to show that under certain assumptions, recurrent neural networks trained to perform a task develop denoising dynamics. 

While there are some interesting ideas presented in this submission, overall it feels that the results presented in the current version are preliminary and not surprising.
reviews.strengths:
— Understanding the neural mechanisms underlying reactions/replays is an important question.

-- The paper seeks to analyze RNNs trained to perform a class of tasks theoretically, which is a somewhat rare excise in this literature. 

— The mathematical analysis of the loss function used in the training and the connection to the Langevin dynamics, while under somewhat strong assumptions, remain interesting.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. A number of relevant studied were not cited and discussed. This include work using RNNs and attractor dynamics to model the replay and theta sequences in the hippocampus, e.g.,
Hopfield, John J. "Neurodynamics of mental exploration." Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 107.4 (2010): 1648-1653.
Kang, Louis, and Michael R. DeWeese. "Replay as wavefronts and theta sequences as bump oscillations in a grid cell attractor network." Elife 8 (2019): e46351.
Chu, Tianhao, et al. "Firing rate adaptation affords place cell theta sweeps, phase precession and procession." bioRxiv (2022): 2022-11.

The paper also misses several pieces work in training RNNs to study the grid cells systems and HD systems.
Cueva, C. J., & Wei, X. X. (2018). Emergence of grid-like representations by training recurrent neural networks to perform spatial localization. ICLR.
Uria, B., Ibarz, B., Banino, A., Zambaldi, V., Kumaran, D., Hassabis, D., Barry, C. and Blundell, C., (2020). The spatial memory pipeline: a model of egocentric to allocentric understanding in mammalian brains. BioRxiv, pp.2020-11.
Cueva, C.J., et al (2020). Emergence of functional and structural properties of the head direction system by optimization of recurrent neural networks.ICLR.

2. The main results are not surprising after considering what we know so far on this topic. Prior work has shown that training the RNNs to perform path integration or angular integration task leads to networks that exhibit attractors dynamics that is similar to continuous attractor models. Furthermore, it is well known that attractor dynamics can perform denoising. Thus it is not surprising that RNNs trained on these previous studied tasks can perform denoising. 

It should also be noted that prior work has further characterize the rate of diffusion along the low-d manifold, e.g., see Fig 6 of the following paper: 
Burak, Y. and Fiete, I.R., 2009. Accurate path integration in continuous attractor network models of grid cells. PLoS computational biology, 5(2), p.e1000291.


3. As described in the paper, the trained network follows diffusion dynamics when the stimulus turned off. This is naturally expected because the diffusion of network state on a low-d manifold. This would result in brownian-motion like trajectory. Furthemore, the replay trajectories observed in the hippocampus typically described by a systematic drift towards one direction (e.g., in linear or circular tracks), not diffusive dynamics. Can the authors be more explicit about the experimental data they were modeling?

4. One more point that confuses me—the authors stated “we found that even trajectories generated by networks that were not trained in the presence of noise, and also were not driven by noise in the quiescent phase, still generated quiescent activity distributions that corresponded well to the active phase distributions. ” This seems to argue against the denoising dynamics, and make the usefulness of the mathematical analysis questionable.

5. The mathematical analysis relied on a set of approximations and assumptions, which were not well justified.
reviews.questions:
Would it be possible to discuss more explicitly how their results are connected or supported by the empirical data? In particular, how is the “diffusive reactivation” related to neural data?

 The mathematical analysis relies on a number of assumptions, to the extent that it is difficult to judge whether the conclusion would actually be applicable to the numerical experiments. Can these assumptions be justified or better motivated (beyond the sake of mathematical convenience)?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:

Can the authors be more explicit about the experimental data they were modeling?

4. One more point that confuses me—the authors stated “we found that even trajectories generated by networks that were not trained in the presence of noise, and also were not driven by noise in the quiescent phase, still generated quiescent activity distributions that corresponded well to the active phase distributions. ” This seems to argue against the denoising dynamics, and make the usefulness of the mathematical analysis questionable.

5. The mathematical analysis relied on a set of approximations and assumptions, which were not well justified.

Questions:
Would it be possible to discuss more explicitly how their results are connected or supported by the empirical data? In particular, how is the “diffusive reactivation” related to neural data?

 The mathematical analysis relies on a number of assumptions, to the extent that it is difficult to judge whether the conclusion would actually be applicable to the numerical experiments. Can these assumptions be justified or better motivated (beyond the sake of mathematical convenience)?

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Modeling Neural Reactivation in Task-Optimized Recurrent Neural Networks"

**Overall Assessment:**  
This paper investigates the emergent property of neural reactivation (replay) in RNNs trained on path integration tasks, proposing that reactivation arises from denoising dynamics inherent to task-optimized networks. While the question of how replay emerges in biological systems is important and the theoretical mathematical analysis is a notable strength, the work is ultimately preliminary. Significant concerns regarding novelty, grounding in prior literature, connection to experimental data, and internal theoretical consistency lead me to recommend **rejection in its current form (Rating: 5 - marginally below the acceptance threshold)**. The core ideas show promise but require substantial revision and strengthening to meet the journal's standards.

**Summary of Strengths:**  
1.  **Important Question:** The paper tackles a significant and timely question regarding the neural mechanisms underlying reactivation and replay phenomena, which are crucial for understanding memory consolidation and planning.
2.  **Theoretical Analysis:** The application of theoretical mathematical analysis to understand the loss function and its potential connection to Langevin dynamics during quiescence is a relatively rare and interesting approach in the RNN literature.
3.  **Task-Optimized RNN Focus:** Studying reactivation as an emergent property in RNNs explicitly trained on biologically relevant tasks (path integration, angular integration) is a valuable direction.

**Summary of Weaknesses and Major Concerns:**  
1.  **Lack of Novelty and Unaddressed Prior Work:** The central claim – that reactivation emerges as a denoising process in task-optimized networks forming low-dimensional attractors – appears insufficiently novel. Crucially, the paper fails to cite and engage with highly relevant prior work that established the foundation for these results. This includes seminal and recent studies on attractor dynamics in neural systems, RNN modeling of grid/HD systems, and replay mechanisms (e.g., Hopfield 2010, Burak & Fiete 2009, Cueva & Wei 2018, Uria et al. 2020, Cueva et al. 2020, Kang & DeWeese 2019, Chu et al. 2022). The finding that such networks develop denoising capabilities leading to diffusive dynamics on a manifold is unsurprising given this established literature.
2.  **Weak Connection to Neural Data:** The concept of "diffusive reactivation" is inadequately linked to experimental neural data. Hippocampal replay, a primary target of this modeling effort, often exhibits systematic drift or structured trajectories, not purely diffusive behavior. The paper does not convincingly explain how the observed diffusion relates to or explains specific features of biological replay phenomena.
3.  **Contradictory Empirical Finding:** A critical technical result undermines the proposed theoretical mechanism. Networks trained *without* any noise and simulated *without* noise during quiescence still generated reactivation activity distributions similar to the active phase. This observation directly contradicts the central hypothesis that reactivation is driven by a denoising process (Langevin dynamics) acting on added noise. This contradiction is not addressed and significantly weakens the core argument.
4.  **Unjustified Mathematical Assumptions:** While the mathematical framework linking the optimized loss function to Langevin sampling dynamics is interesting, it relies on several strong approximations and assumptions (e.g., specific forms of the loss, noise characteristics). These assumptions are not sufficiently motivated beyond mathematical convenience, and their biological plausibility or relevance to the actual network dynamics observed in the simulations is questionable. The analysis feels disconnected from the numerical results, especially given point #3.
5.  **Preliminary Nature:** The results feel preliminary. The analysis of reactivation is limited, and the connection between the theory and the simulations, particularly regarding the denoising mechanism, is not convincingly established.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1.  **Integrate Relevant Literature:** Thoroughly discuss and cite the missing prior work on attractor networks, RNN models of spatial navigation systems (grid/HD), and replay mechanisms. Explicitly position the current work within this context, clarifying what specific novel insight it provides beyond existing knowledge.
2.  **Strengthen Connection to Biology:** Explicitly link the model's "diffusive reactivation" findings to specific experimental neural data on replay. Discuss how the model's behavior relates to (or differs from) observed replay phenomena (e.g., structured trajectories vs. diffusion) and what biological implications the diffusive model might have.
3.  **Address Theoretical Contradiction:** Provide a clear explanation and discussion for the critical finding that reactivation occurs even in networks trained and tested *without* noise. This result fundamentally challenges the proposed denoising/Langevin mechanism and must be resolved or its implications thoroughly discussed to salvage the theoretical framework.
4.  **Justify Mathematical Assumptions:** Provide stronger biological or theoretical motivation for the key assumptions and approximations used in the mathematical analysis. Discuss their limitations and potential impact on the conclusions. Better bridge the gap between the mathematical theory and the simulation results.
5.  **Deepen Analysis:** Expand the analysis of the reactivation dynamics, potentially exploring conditions under which more structured (non-diffusive) replay might emerge in such networks, or analyzing the relationship between task structure and the nature of the reactivation manifold.

**Recommendation:**  
**Reject (Rating: 5 - marginally below the acceptance threshold).** The paper addresses an important question and employs a potentially valuable theoretical approach. However, the concerns regarding novelty (due to unaddressed prior work), the weak connection to experimental neural data, the critical internal contradiction regarding the noise-free reactivation, and the insufficient justification of key mathematical assumptions are substantial. The work requires significant revision and strengthening, particularly in contextualization, biological grounding, and theoretical consistency, before it would be suitable for publication. The core idea has merit, and I encourage the authors to address these points thoroughly in a future submission.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This submission seeks to model the reactivation of brain activity during periods of quiescence using task-optimized network. The paper develops some mathematical formalisms to show that under certain assumptions, recurrent neural networks trained to perform a task develop denoising dynamics. 

While there are some interesting ideas presented in this submission, overall it feels that the results presented in the current version are preliminary and not surprising.

Strengths:
— Understanding the neural mechanisms underlying reactions/replays is an important question.

-- The paper seeks to analyze RNNs trained to perform a class of tasks theoretically, which is a somewhat rare excise in this literature. 

— The mathematical analysis of the loss function used in the training and the connection to the Langevin dynamics, while under somewhat strong assumptions, remain interesting.

Weaknesses:
1. A number of relevant studied were not cited and discussed. This include work using RNNs and attractor dynamics to model the replay and theta sequences in the hippocampus, e.g.,
Hopfield, John J. "Neurodynamics of mental exploration." Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 107.4 (2010): 1648-1653.
Kang, Louis, and Michael R. DeWeese. "Replay as wavefronts and theta sequences as bump oscillations in a grid cell attractor network." Elife 8 (2019): e46351.
Chu, Tianhao, et al. "Firing rate adaptation affords place cell theta sweeps, phase precession and procession." bioRxiv (2022): 2022-11.

The paper also misses several pieces work in training RNNs to study the grid cells systems and HD systems.
Cueva, C. J., & Wei, X. X. (2018). Emergence of grid-like representations by training recurrent neural networks to perform spatial localization. ICLR.
Uria, B., Ibarz, B., Banino, A., Zambaldi, V., Kumaran, D., Hassabis, D., Barry, C. and Blundell, C., (2020). The spatial memory pipeline: a model of egocentric to allocentric understanding in mammalian brains. BioRxiv, pp.2020-11.
Cueva, C.J., et al (2020). Emergence of functional and structural properties of the head direction system by optimization of recurrent neural networks.ICLR.

2. The main results are not surprising after considering what we know so far on this topic. Prior work has shown that training the RNNs to perform path integration or angular integration task leads to networks that exhibit attractors dynamics that is similar to continuous attractor models. Furthermore, it is well known that attractor dynamics can perform denoising. Thus it is not surprising that RNNs trained on these previous studied tasks can perform denoising. 

It should also be noted that prior work has further characterize the rate of diffusion along the low-d manifold, e.g., see Fig 6 of the following paper: 
Burak, Y. and Fiete, I.R., 2009. Accurate path integration in continuous attractor network models of grid cells. PLoS computational biology, 5(2), p.e1000291.


3. As described in the paper, the trained network follows diffusion dynamics when the stimulus turned off. This is naturally expected because the diffusion of network state on a low-d manifold. This would result in brownian-motion like trajectory. Furthemore, the replay trajectories observed in the hippocampus typically described by a systematic drift towards one direction (e.g., in linear or circular tracks), not diffusive dynamics. Can the authors be more explicit about the experimental data they were modeling?

4. One more point that confuses me—the authors stated “we found that even trajectories generated by networks that were not trained in the presence of noise, and also were not driven by noise in the quiescent phase, still generated quiescent activity distributions that corresponded well to the active phase distributions. ” This seems to argue against the denoising dynamics, and make the usefulness of the mathematical analysis questionable.

5. The mathematical analysis relied on a set of approximations and assumptions, which were not well justified.

Questions:
Would it be possible to discuss more explicitly how their results are connected or supported by the empirical data? In particular, how is the “diffusive reactivation” related to neural data?

 The mathematical analysis relies on a number of assumptions, to the extent that it is difficult to judge whether the conclusion would actually be applicable to the numerical experiments. Can these assumptions be justified or better motivated (beyond the sake of mathematical convenience)?

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Boosting Graph Anomaly Detection with Adaptive Message Passing

reviews.summary: The authors propose a model for unsupervised anomaly detection in graphs. In particular, the model focuses on 2 types of anomalies: 1) contextual (a node having attributes that are very different from its neighbors') and 2) structural (dense subgraphs). The innovation is a two-stage model that first uses an MLP to find possible contextual anomalies and then uses a message-passing graph neural network with an attention mechanism to find dense subgraphs of anomalous nodes.
reviews.strengths:
- Takes inspiration of previous work on contrastive learning and recent work on attention for message passing 
- Competitive time complexity
- Experimental evaluation is fairly comprehensive and shows modest improvement over existing methods, of which there are plenty
reviews.weaknesses:
- Performance reported for competing methods is lower than in the papers where these methods were proposed. It raises the question of whether the models are sufficiently tuned
- No comparison to methods in the vast literature outside deep learning / neural networks. See for example https://arxiv.org/abs/1404.4679
reviews.questions:
- The AnomalyDAE paper reports an AUC of over 97 for the BlogCatalog dataset. Do you have an explanation for the discrepancy in your paper (AUC of 76.58)? Were the authors of that paper injecting anomalies in a totally different way?

- The AUC for Sub-CR, DOMINANT, and  COLA is higher in the Sub-CR paper. Do you think hyperparameters for the baseline models are sufficiently tuned?
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors propose a model for unsupervised anomaly detection in graphs. In particular, the model focuses on 2 types of anomalies: 1) contextual (a node having attributes that are very different from its neighbors') and 2) structural (dense subgraphs). The innovation is a two-stage model that first uses an MLP to find possible contextual anomalies and then uses a message-passing graph neural network with an attention mechanism to find dense subgraphs of anomalous nodes.

Strengths:
- Takes inspiration of previous work on contrastive learning and recent work on attention for message passing 
- Competitive time complexity
- Experimental evaluation is fairly comprehensive and shows modest improvement over existing methods, of which there are plenty

Weaknesses:
- Performance reported for competing methods is lower than in the papers where these methods were proposed. It raises the question of whether the models are sufficiently tuned
- No comparison to methods in the vast literature outside deep learning / neural networks. See for example https://arxiv.org/abs/1404.4679

Questions:
- The AnomalyDAE paper reports an AUC of over 97 for the BlogCatalog dataset. Do you have an explanation for the discrepancy in your paper (AUC of 76.58)? Were the authors of that paper injecting anomalies in a totally different way?

- The AUC for Sub-CR, DOMINANT, and  COLA is higher in the Sub-CR paper. Do you think hyperparameters for the baseline models are sufficiently tuned?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## New Peer Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a two-stage unsupervised framework for graph anomaly detection, targeting both contextual and structural anomalies. The method employs an MLP for initial contextual anomaly scoring, followed by a message-passing GNN with a novel hybrid attention mechanism for structural refinement and final scoring. While the approach demonstrates modest performance improvements over existing deep learning baselines across multiple datasets, concerns regarding baseline replication and the scope of comparative evaluation temper the significance of these gains.  

### **Strengths:**  
1. **Novel Architectural Integration:** The two-stage design effectively combines an MLP’s feature-learning capability with a GNN’s structural awareness. The hybrid attention mechanism (leveraging anomaly score differences and feature similarities) is a well-motivated innovation building on prior contrastive learning and attention literature.  
2. **Computational Efficiency:** The model’s competitive time complexity is a practical advantage, making it scalable for real-world graph applications.  
3. **Rigorous Experimental Setup:** The comprehensive evaluation across multiple benchmark datasets and inclusion of numerous deep learning baselines strengthens the empirical validation.  

### **Weaknesses and Concerns:**  
1. **Baseline Performance Discrepancies:** Reported results for key baselines (e.g., AnomalyDAE’s AUC on BlogCatalog) are notably lower than those in their original publications. This raises significant concerns about the adequacy of hyperparameter tuning or implementation fidelity for comparative methods, potentially undermining the claimed improvements of the proposed model.  
2. **Limited Evaluation Scope:** The absence of comparisons to established non-neural or shallow methods (e.g., proximity-based, statistical, or matrix factorization techniques) from the broader anomaly detection literature restricts the assessment of the method’s general advancement. Relying solely on deep learning baselines does not fully contextualize its contribution.  

### **Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1. **Address Baseline Performance Gaps:** Provide a detailed explanation for the observed discrepancies in baseline performance (e.g., specific hyperparameter settings, data preprocessing differences, or validation protocols used). Justify the tuning strategy employed for all baselines to ensure a fair comparison.  
2. **Expand Comparative Analysis:** Include results from representative non-deep learning anomaly detection methods (e.g., LOF, SCAN, or A^3) to demonstrate the model’s broader relevance and robustness.  
3. **Clarify Hyperparameter Sensitivity:** Discuss the sensitivity of the proposed model to key hyperparameters (e.g., attention mechanism design choices) and how robust tuning was achieved.  

### **Overall Assessment:**  
The paper presents a technically sound approach with a well-motivated two-stage architecture and a useful hybrid attention mechanism. The comprehensive evaluation against neural baselines is commendable, and the modest empirical gains are promising. However, the unresolved discrepancies in baseline performance and the narrow scope of comparative methods cast doubt on the magnitude of the claimed contribution. **While the work is marginally above the acceptance threshold due to its methodological novelty and thorough neural benchmark evaluation**, addressing the above concerns is essential to solidify its validity and impact.  

**Recommendation:**  
**Accept (Marginally)**  
*The paper meets the acceptance threshold but requires revisions to address validation concerns, particularly regarding baseline comparisons.*

Summary:
The authors propose a model for unsupervised anomaly detection in graphs. In particular, the model focuses on 2 types of anomalies: 1) contextual (a node having attributes that are very different from its neighbors') and 2) structural (dense subgraphs). The innovation is a two-stage model that first uses an MLP to find possible contextual anomalies and then uses a message-passing graph neural network with an attention mechanism to find dense subgraphs of anomalous nodes.

Strengths:
- Takes inspiration of previous work on contrastive learning and recent work on attention for message passing

- Experimental evaluation is fairly comprehensive and shows modest improvement over existing methods, of which there are plenty

Weaknesses:

Questions:

- The AUC for Sub-CR, DOMINANT, and  COLA is higher in the Sub-CR paper. Do you think hyperparameters for the baseline models are sufficiently tuned?

Rating:

reviews.summary: The authors investigate the two main paradigms, both used in many unsupervised graph anomaly detection models, message passings (MP) and local inconsistency mining (LIM). Traditionally, many graph anomaly detection models utilize the LIM for determining nodes that have inconsistency with their surroundings and mark them as anomalous. However, the use of MP with many GNN layers tends to smooth out everything, which decreases the contrastive difference between a node and its surroundings. The authors aim to address this conflict by proposing GADAM, a method that first performs LIM via a regular MLP network to get local anomaly scores without using message passing. It then combines these local scores with global scores that use an attention-based adaptive message passing that enables nodes to selectively absorb normal/abnormal signals from their surroundings. The authors then demonstrate the benefit of the models compared with baselines on several public datasets.
reviews.strengths:
Strengths:
- An interesting approach in unsupervised anomaly detection. The over-smoothing problem is a well-known problem in GNN, and it may impact the performance of GNN-based anomaly detection models. Most unsupervised graph anomaly detection models work by finding inconsistent nodes among the neighbors. The use of message passing in GNN may impact the finding as the over-smoothing problem may make the nodes less inconsistent.
I would argue that the conflict between LIM and overs-smoothing could be resolved within a message-passing framework by designing a better message-passing flow, imposing stricter bottlenecks in the encoder-decoder process, or other means. However, the paper proposed a different way outside the message-passing framework.

- The methods combine both local perspective and global perspective to create combined perspective anomaly scores.
- The authors demonstrate the benefit of the model against many GNN baselines
reviews.weaknesses:
I have a few concerns and questions regarding the paper:
1) In the contrastive pair construction (Section 3.1 item (2)), the authors mentioned that the method uses the complete adjacency subgraph. Does it mean that it includes all 1-hop neighbors? If that's the case, then the receptive field of the method is limited to just the immediate neighbors. This may limit the expressiveness of the model. There may be cases where determining normal/abnormal nodes requires more than just looking at the immediate neighbors. 
2) If in (1), it is not just 1-hop neighbors but all k-hop neighbors, then the size of the subgraphs can be potentially very large in densely connected graphs. How does the model address that?
3) In the shuffling process, there are some chances that neighboring nodes are accidentally picked as negative pairs. How does the model account for those cases?
4) The representations of a subgraph G in the local context are just an average of the representation of the node embeddings in G. Then, the anomaly scores for each node are measured by the difference between its node representation and the subgraph representation. It is possible that the node embedding of each neighbor is really different than the target node's embedding, but the average of the neighbor embeddings is similar to the target node. Doesn't it also introduce an over-smoothing problem? It could be worse than the standard message passing, where the aggregation is not just standard averaging but parameterized by a weight matrix. Here, the aggregation is just a plain average.
5) The use of attention mechanisms may be counter-intuitive in unsupervised anomaly detection that is based on finding abnormal signals from the surroundings. The ability of the attention mechanism to ignore certain signals may make the model ignore suspicious signals, which will result in an abnormal case tagged as a normal case. Has the author tried to replace the attention mechanism with another mechanism, like convolution, that forces the model to utilize all signals?
6) The experiment results of the baselines presented in the paper are rather different from the ones reported in the original paper for the same datasets. For example, in the original AnomalyDAE paper, the AUC are 97.81 (BlogCatalog), 90.05 (ACM); whereas in this paper, they are 76.58 (BlogCatalog), 75/16 (ACM). Could the authors explain more about it?
7) How to decide topk% included in the second stage. How does the selection of this percentage affect the results?
8) The paper is missing some unsupervised graph anomaly detection papers:
     - Ding, Kaize, Jundong Li, Nitin Agarwal, and Huan Liu. "Inductive anomaly detection on attributed networks." In Proceedings of the twenty-ninth international conference on international joint conferences on artificial intelligence, pp. 1288-1294. 2021.
     - Huang, Yihong, Liping Wang, Fan Zhang, and Xuemin Lin. "Are we really making much progress in unsupervised graph outlier detection? Revisiting the problem with new insight and superior method." arXiv preprint arXiv:2210.12941 (2022).
     - Yang, Shujie, Binchi Zhang, Shangbin Feng, Zhanxuan Tan, Qinghua Zheng, Jun Zhou, and Minnan Luo. "Ahead: A triple attention based heterogeneous graph anomaly detection approach." In Chinese Intelligent Automation Conference, pp. 542-552. Singapore: Springer Nature Singapore, 2023.
     - Fathony, Rizal, Jenn Ng, and Jia Chen. "Interaction-Focused Anomaly Detection on Bipartite Node-and-Edge-Attributed Graphs." In 2023 International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN), pp. 1-10. IEEE, 2023.
     - Wang, Qizhou, Guansong Pang, Mahsa Salehi, Wray Buntine, and Christopher Leckie. "Cross-domain graph anomaly detection via anomaly-aware contrastive alignment." In Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, vol. 37, no. 4, pp. 4676-4684. 2023.
reviews.questions: Please answer my questions in the previous section.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors investigate the two main paradigms, both used in many unsupervised graph anomaly detection models, message passings (MP) and local inconsistency mining (LIM). Traditionally, many graph anomaly detection models utilize the LIM for determining nodes that have inconsistency with their surroundings and mark them as anomalous. However, the use of MP with many GNN layers tends to smooth out everything, which decreases the contrastive difference between a node and its surroundings. The authors aim to address this conflict by proposing GADAM, a method that first performs LIM via a regular MLP network to get local anomaly scores without using message passing. It then combines these local scores with global scores that use an attention-based adaptive message passing that enables nodes to selectively absorb normal/abnormal signals from their surroundings. The authors then demonstrate the benefit of the models compared with baselines on several public datasets.

Strengths:
Strengths:
- An interesting approach in unsupervised anomaly detection. The over-smoothing problem is a well-known problem in GNN, and it may impact the performance of GNN-based anomaly detection models. Most unsupervised graph anomaly detection models work by finding inconsistent nodes among the neighbors. The use of message passing in GNN may impact the finding as the over-smoothing problem may make the nodes less inconsistent.
I would argue that the conflict between LIM and overs-smoothing could be resolved within a message-passing framework by designing a better message-passing flow, imposing stricter bottlenecks in the encoder-decoder process, or other means. However, the paper proposed a different way outside the message-passing framework.

- The methods combine both local perspective and global perspective to create combined perspective anomaly scores.
- The authors demonstrate the benefit of the model against many GNN baselines

Weaknesses:
I have a few concerns and questions regarding the paper:
1) In the contrastive pair construction (Section 3.1 item (2)), the authors mentioned that the method uses the complete adjacency subgraph. Does it mean that it includes all 1-hop neighbors? If that's the case, then the receptive field of the method is limited to just the immediate neighbors. This may limit the expressiveness of the model. There may be cases where determining normal/abnormal nodes requires more than just looking at the immediate neighbors. 
2) If in (1), it is not just 1-hop neighbors but all k-hop neighbors, then the size of the subgraphs can be potentially very large in densely connected graphs. How does the model address that?
3) In the shuffling process, there are some chances that neighboring nodes are accidentally picked as negative pairs. How does the model account for those cases?
4) The representations of a subgraph G in the local context are just an average of the representation of the node embeddings in G. Then, the anomaly scores for each node are measured by the difference between its node representation and the subgraph representation. It is possible that the node embedding of each neighbor is really different than the target node's embedding, but the average of the neighbor embeddings is similar to the target node. Doesn't it also introduce an over-smoothing problem? It could be worse than the standard message passing, where the aggregation is not just standard averaging but parameterized by a weight matrix. Here, the aggregation is just a plain average.
5) The use of attention mechanisms may be counter-intuitive in unsupervised anomaly detection that is based on finding abnormal signals from the surroundings. The ability of the attention mechanism to ignore certain signals may make the model ignore suspicious signals, which will result in an abnormal case tagged as a normal case. Has the author tried to replace the attention mechanism with another mechanism, like convolution, that forces the model to utilize all signals?
6) The experiment results of the baselines presented in the paper are rather different from the ones reported in the original paper for the same datasets. For example, in the original AnomalyDAE paper, the AUC are 97.81 (BlogCatalog), 90.05 (ACM); whereas in this paper, they are 76.58 (BlogCatalog), 75/16 (ACM). Could the authors explain more about it?
7) How to decide topk% included in the second stage. How does the selection of this percentage affect the results?
8) The paper is missing some unsupervised graph anomaly detection papers:
     - Ding, Kaize, Jundong Li, Nitin Agarwal, and Huan Liu. "Inductive anomaly detection on attributed networks." In Proceedings of the twenty-ninth international conference on international joint conferences on artificial intelligence, pp. 1288-1294. 2021.
     - Huang, Yihong, Liping Wang, Fan Zhang, and Xuemin Lin. "Are we really making much progress in unsupervised graph outlier detection? Revisiting the problem with new insight and superior method." arXiv preprint arXiv:2210.12941 (2022).
     - Yang, Shujie, Binchi Zhang, Shangbin Feng, Zhanxuan Tan, Qinghua Zheng, Jun Zhou, and Minnan Luo. "Ahead: A triple attention based heterogeneous graph anomaly detection approach." In Chinese Intelligent Automation Conference, pp. 542-552. Singapore: Springer Nature Singapore, 2023.
     - Fathony, Rizal, Jenn Ng, and Jia Chen. "Interaction-Focused Anomaly Detection on Bipartite Node-and-Edge-Attributed Graphs." In 2023 International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN), pp. 1-10. IEEE, 2023.
     - Wang, Qizhou, Guansong Pang, Mahsa Salehi, Wray Buntine, and Christopher Leckie. "Cross-domain graph anomaly detection via anomaly-aware contrastive alignment." In Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, vol. 37, no. 4, pp. 4676-4684. 2023.

Questions:
